critique.summary: Pyodide is a Python distribution that runs in the browser and Node.js using WebAssembly. It allows running Python code and packages, including those with C, C++, and Rust extensions, directly in web environments with a robust JavaScript-Python interface.
mainPoints: Pyodide is a port of CPython to WebAssembly/Emscripten.
It supports installing and running Python packages in the browser via micropip, including many scientific and general-purpose packages.
Pyodide provides a JavaScript-Python foreign function interface with support for error handling and async features.
Python code running in the browser via Pyodide has full access to Web APIs.
The project originated in 2018 at Mozilla as part of the Iodide project and is now community-driven and open source.
Pyodide is licensed under the Mozilla Public License 2.0.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Pyodide's capabilities and origins are accurate and consistent with information available from the official Pyodide website and GitHub repository. Pyodide is indeed a CPython port to WebAssembly that enables running Python in browsers and Node.js, supporting many packages including those with native extensions. The project's history linked to Mozilla and Iodide is also verifiable. The description of features such as the JavaScript-Python interface and Web API access aligns with documented functionality.
considerations: Performance limitations of running Python in WebAssembly compared to native execution are not discussed.
Security implications of running Python code in the browser environment are not mentioned.
The article does not address compatibility issues or limitations with certain Python packages or extensions.
The need for network access or hosting considerations for Pyodide distributions is not covered.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion highlights Pyodide as a mature and versatile Python distribution running in the browser via WebAssembly, praised for enabling Python-based web UIs and teaching tools. Some comments note its strengths in privacy and functionality compared to other solutions, while others mention performance trade-offs when using heavy libraries like numpy.

comments.author: simonw
comments.text:
Pyodide is one of the hidden gems of the Python ecosystem. It's SO good at what it does, and it's nearly 8 years old now so it's pretty mature.

I love using Pyodide to build web UIs for trying out new Python libraries. Here's one I built a few weeks ago to exercise my pure-Python SQLite AST parser, for example: https://tools.simonwillison.net/sqlite-ast

It's also pretty easy[1] to get C or Rust libraries that have Python bindings compiled to a WebAssembly wheel that Pyodide can then load.

Here's a bit of a nutty example - the new Monty Python-like sandbox library (written in Rust) compiled to WASM and then loaded in Pyodide in the browser: https://simonw.github.io/research/monty-wasm-pyodide/pyodide...

[1] OK, Claude Code knows how to do it.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and accurate overview of Pyodide's maturity, use cases, and technical capabilities, supported by concrete examples and links.

comments.author: fzumstein
comments.text: Pyodide powers xlwings Lite, a free Excel add-in that you can install from Excel’s add-in store with a single click. It outperforms Microsoft’s official Python in Excel solution in every coceivable way: price, privacy, speed, can install packages, can access the internet, can access local files, doesn’t have a usage quota, can automate Excel and create native UDFs. See https://lite.xlwings.org
comments.analysis: The comment accurately describes a real-world application of Pyodide with specific advantages over Microsoft's solution, though some claims about performance could vary by context.

comments.author: devsda
comments.text:
There's also xeus-cpp which is cpp based on wasm. Xeus-cpp and pyodide are the backends for Jupyterlite [1] kernels in browser.

It's actually a very good way to teach coding in python/c++(11, 17 or even 23), explore language features or your own library within browser and allow students to execute code by hosting just some static html pages and some assets without any backend.

1. https://jupyter.org/try-jupyter/lab/
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies related technologies and their educational benefits, providing a helpful context for Pyodide's ecosystem.

comments.author: QuadmasterXLII
comments.text: It works surprisingly well in terms of writing python for native execution, and then trying to share on the web and having it Just Work TM. Unfortunately, when I want python it's because I want numpy, scipy and friends, and once you bring them on board pyodide load times are long. As an example comparison: a face turning octahedron puzzle in python + pyodide, loads in 10 seconds https://cubes.hgreer.com/fto.html . Meanwhile, a megaminx puzzle in javascript (statically generated by python) loads in 200 ms https://cubes.hgreer.com/ssg/output.html
comments.analysis: The comment offers a practical performance critique backed by examples, highlighting a known limitation of Pyodide with heavy scientific libraries.

comments.author: mcintyre1994
comments.text:
Love all the tools mentioned in the comments, Pyodide is a fun tool. I built https://pyground.vercel.app with it a few years ago, as a quick way to run Python on a CSV/JSON file locally in the browser. For a while Python/Matplotlib was what I was most comfortable writing quick and simple analysis scripts with, and this was my go to for one-off data analysis work. You can drag-drop a file in, then some 'clever' code makes it available as `data` in the Python script so you don't have to write any parsing code yourself.

Now I'd probably just get Claude Code to write something locally, but there might be some value in a version of pyground that can use an LLM to write the Python code for you (and give it the data shape) but keeps the local execution setup the same.
comments.analysis: This personal experience adds value by showing a practical use case and future potential, though the LLM integration is speculative.

comments.author: screamingninja
comments.text:
https://marimo.app/

A reactive notebook for Python that runs in browser, powered by Pyodide. I have been using it extensively for teaching Python to non-STEM students that do not need the added friction of navigating the CLI or filesystem hierarchy.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a relevant example of Pyodide's educational use, emphasizing accessibility benefits for beginners.

comments.author: ianberdin
comments.text:
Yea, I even built online ide based on Pyodide. Insane peace of software. 
You can try here: playcode.io/python
comments.analysis: While enthusiastic and brief, this comment confirms Pyodide's capability for building complex tools like IDEs, though it lacks detailed technical insight.

comments.author: jasiek
comments.text: I've used it to put together a browser app to program walkie-talkies: https://codeplug.org
comments.analysis: A straightforward real-world application example, lending credibility to Pyodide's versatility but without deeper analysis.

comments.author: ipunchghosts
comments.text:
REMUS RLF reader runs pyodide to parse underwater drone mission files in the browser

https://isaacgerg.github.io/remus-rlf-reader/webapp/
comments.analysis: Another practical use case demonstrating Pyodide's utility in niche domains, supporting its adaptability.

comments.author: yawnxyz
comments.text: no but it works great in conjunction with observable and d3js for pretty interactives!
comments.analysis: A brief but accurate comment about Pyodide's compatibility with popular visualization tools, though lacking detail.

comments.author: jacob019
comments.text: Anyone using it with nodejs to make a sandbox for code agents?
comments.analysis: This is a question rather than a factual comment, contributing little to the analysis of Pyodide's capabilities.

comments.author: yjftsjthsd-h
comments.text: That is very cool. Has anyone made a nice beginner's learning environment out of it? Seems like it would solve some of the extra friction that makes it hard to get started.
comments.analysis: This is a query seeking information rather than providing insights or analysis.

comments.author: williamstein
comments.text: I tried to make a version of this using Zig once, but ran out of steam: https://cowasm.org/
comments.analysis: While related to WebAssembly, this comment is anecdotal and does not add meaningful analysis about Pyodide.

comments.author: Archit3ch
comments.analysis: This is a wishful statement without factual content or analysis.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text: WebAssembly will reach a breakthrough in the same year as GNU Hurd will dominate, together with the Desktop-Linux-of-the-year.
comments.analysis: This comment is sarcastic and unrealistic, undermining its credibility and relevance.
